coredata.dc:title: A smartphone-coalesced nanoprobe for high selective ammonia sensing based on the pH-responsive biomass carbon nanodots and headspace single drop microextraction
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Fan, Yu Zhu
Dong, Jiang Xue
Zhang, Ying
Li, Na
Liu, Shi Gang
Geng, Shuo
Ling, Yu
Luo, Hong Qun
Li, Nian Bing
coredata.dc:description: Ammonia concentration together with pH values are important and closely linked indexes for aqueous systems. Rapid on-site determination of ammonia or pH is of great significance to environmental monitoring. In this work, a pH-switchable nanoprobe based on biomass carbon dots (CDs) is developed using a smartphone as a simple and handy instrument. The CDs demonstrate sensitive pH response in wide linear ranges of 6.1–13.6, and 2.0–13.6 with colorimetric and fluorescent channels, respectively. It is the pH-induced aggregation that governs the color and fluorescence switch. With the pH evolution caused by the dissolution of ammonia, the smartphone-integrated nanoprobe is applied to ammonia detection with a broad range of 0.5–300 mM. Moreover, the headspace single drop microextraction strategy can concentrate ammonia from matrix, offering a remarkably high selectivity for ammonia determination. Finally, the practical applications of this method for ammonia analysis obtained satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Smartphone
Carbon dots
Colorimetry
Ammonia
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optimization and process of CDs formation
Smartphone-coalesced colorimetric nanoprobe for wide-range pH sensing
Fluorescence-channel nanoprobe for wide-range pH sensing
Dual-channel nanoprobe to wide-range ammonia detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image, (B) HRTEM image, and (C) particle size distribution of CDs. (D) FTIR, (E) 1H NMR, (F) XPS spectra of CDs and high-resolution spectra of C1s (G), N1s (H), and O1s (I) of the CDs.

(A) The absorption (Abs), fluorescence excitation (Ex), and emission (Em) spectra of CDs. Insets: photographs taken (a) under visible light and (b) under 365 nm UV light. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs with excitation of different wavelengths. (C) Scheme for the formation of CDs. C (CDs) = 12 μL/mL.

(A) Photographs (up) and the corresponding smartphone-vivified color graphs (down) of CDs in different pH values (6.1–13.6) under visible light. (B) Screen capture of the running ColorColl app of iPhone 7plue. (C) Plot of V vs. pH values. Inset: Linear plot of V as a function of the pH. (D) The UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs with different pH values (pH = 2.0–13.6). (E) Plot of the relative absorbance versus pH. Conditions: C (CDs) = 10 μL/mL; C (Rhodamine S) = 2.58 μg/mL.

(A) TEM images and (B) hydrodynamic sizes of CDs under pH 2.0, 6.0, and 12.0, respectively. (C) Hydrodynamic sizes of CDs in buffers of different pH values. (D) UV−vis absorption spectra of CDs at pH 2.0 and 13.6 (inset: Photographs of CDs at different pH values under visible light).
Mechanism of the smartphone-coalesced colorimetric assay for pH sensing by CDs.

(A) Histogram of the fluorescence intensity of CDs versus pH (pH = 2.0–13.6, λ ex = 380 nm, λ em = 460 nm). (B, D) Fluorescence emission spectra, emission wavelengths and fluorescence intensities (insets of B, D) of CDs with different pH values (B: pH = 2.0–6.1; D: pH = 6.1–13.6). (C, E) Corresponding linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and pH values. (F) Fluorescence intensity upon the cyclic switching of CDs under alternating conditions of pH 6.0 and 11.0 (inset: Corresponding photographs under ultraviolet light).

(A) Schematic illustration about ammonia sensing combined with HS-SDME strategy. (B) Photographs of CDs with addition of different concentrations of NH4Cl (0–300 mM) under visible light. (C) Optical images of the CDs translated with the smartphone with different concentrations of ammonia. (D) Linear relationship of the intensity V versus the ammonia concentration from 0.5 to 300 mM. (E) Fluorescence spectra of CDs with different concentrations of NH4Cl at 380 nm excitation. (F) Fluorescence quenching intensity (F 0
F) of CDs by NH4Cl with different concentrations. Inset: plot of (F 0
F) versus NH4Cl concentration from 0.1 to 50 mM. (G, H) Selectivity of CDs solution for NH4Cl over species. F 0 and F denote the fluorescence intensity of CDs in the absence and presence of various ions, respectively. Conditions: C (CDs) = 10 μL/mL; C (NH4Cl) = 10 mM; C (interferences) = 50 mM; λ ex = 380 nm; λ em = 460 nm.

head.ce:title: A smartphone-coalesced nanoprobe for high selective ammonia sensing based on the pH-responsive biomass carbon nanodots and headspace single drop microextraction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Eco-Environments in Three Gorges Reservoir Region (Ministry of Education), School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Eco-Environments in Three Gorges Reservoir Region (Ministry of Education)
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Key Laboratory of Green Catalysis of Higher Education Institutes of Sichuan, Sichuan University of Science and Engineering, Zigong 643000, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering
Key Laboratory of Green Catalysis of Higher Education Institutes of Sichuan
Sichuan University of Science and Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Tiansheng Road, BeiBei District, Chongqing 400715, People's Republic of China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ammonia concentration together with pH values are important and closely linked indexes for aqueous systems. Rapid on-site determination of ammonia or pH is of great significance to environmental monitoring. In this work, a pH-switchable nanoprobe based on biomass carbon dots (CDs) is developed using a smartphone as a simple and handy instrument. The CDs demonstrate sensitive pH response in wide linear ranges of 6.1–13.6, and 2.0–13.6 with colorimetric and fluorescent channels, respectively. It is the pH-induced aggregation that governs the color and fluorescence switch. With the pH evolution caused by the dissolution of ammonia, the smartphone-integrated nanoprobe is applied to ammonia detection with a broad range of 0.5–300 mM. Moreover, the headspace single drop microextraction strategy can concentrate ammonia from matrix, offering a remarkably high selectivity for ammonia determination. Finally, the practical applications of this method for ammonia analysis obtained satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: The detection of pH and its variations has been a research theme for a long time because it is very important for the environment and life forms. In terms of the environment, acid rain and raw sewage can cause soil and water contamination, which manifest themselves as the change of pH values [1]. In addition, almost all structure and function maintaining of proteins rely on pH values [2–4]. Thus, the accurate estimation of pH values is essential for the environmental and biological monitoring.

Optical method such as fluorometry and UV–vis spectrophotometry possesses various merits including rapid response, good sensitivity, high signal-to-noise ratio, and noninvasiveness [5,6]. Meanwhile, colorimetry has been widely utilized for pH sensing because of its simplicity, general applicability, and convenient readout with naked eye [7,8]. Yet some limitations still exist. Naked-eye colorimetry allows for prominent simplicity and portability, but is deficient in precision and quantization. Simple chromometric methods cannot afford to accurate quantitative detection, due to the limited color distinguishing ability of naked eyes. Spectrophotometry enables quantitative analysis, but demands bulky equipment, time-consuming procedures. Thus, a portable and accurate signal reader is necessary.

As a handy intelligent tool for everyone, smartphone is not just a tool for communication. The excellent intelligence and portability of phone endow it expectable potential for versatile detector [9,10]. Combined with a technical application (app), smartphone can accurate capture the color variety, and translate with numerical values. It enables accurate and sensitive pH quantitation compared to traditional naked-eye chromometry [11]. Furthermore, the portable analyzer and time-saving procedures make it potential for on-site pH quantitation. In addition, much effort has been devoted to exploring effective nanomaterial-based assay for pH sensing. As a fascinating member of carbon nanomaterials family, carbon dots (CDs) have got tremendous attentions because of their favorable attributes. Their outstanding optical properties, good photochemical stability, eco-friendliness, and good biocompatibility promote the versatile applications of CDs in many fields [12,13].

Ammonia concentration together with pH values are important and closely linked indexes for aqueous systems. The dissolution of ammonia would cause pH change. Excess emissions of ammonia could result in eutrophication of aquatic environment and damage of aquatic ecosystems [14]. Moreover, drinking ammonia-containing water for a long time can cause health risks, because ammonia can be oxidized to cancerogenic nitrate by nitrobacteria [15]. Thus, the accurate estimations of ammonia are essential for the environmental and biological monitoring. However, some dissolved alkaline species may interfere with the measurements. In order to improve the selectivity of ammonia sensing, the headspace single drop microextraction (HS-SDME) strategy is advisable. This technique extracts and concentrates targets from Complex matrix, offering high selectivity, operational simplicity, and less reagents consumption [16,17].

In this paper, a smartphone-coalesced dual-channel nanoprobe for wide-range pH and high selective ammonia sensing based on biomass CDs was developed. The CDs were prepared by green hydrothermal route using onion juice as the precursor, and show good optical properties and superior stability. In addition, the CDs-based nanoprobe combined with the smartphone possesses the following merits. First, unlike the traditional visual colorimetry, the smartphone-integrated transducer can achieve colorimetric quantitation of pH, endowing the method with portability and precision. Second, the sensing platform is provided with switchable colorimetric/fluorescent properties based on pH variations. Third, the color, absorbance, and fluorescence intensity of CDs demonstrate sensitive responses to pH variation with wide working ranges. The origin is that pH-induced aggregation governs the sensitive color and fluorescence switch. Fourth, combined with the HS-SDME technique, the CDs-based dual-channel assay displays good wide-range fluorescent and UV–vis absorption responses toward ammonia, which has a certain guiding significance for the prevention and control of moderate and severe ammonia pollution.

CDs were prepared by a hydrothermal route with onion juice. In a typical procedure, an onion was cut into small pieces and ground using a mortar and pestle. Then 5.0 mL of the onion juice was transferred into a 20 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at a constant temperature of 200 °C for 6 h. After the reaction was completed, a dark brown liquid was obtained. Next, the dark brown solution was centrifuged (10,000 rpm) for 15 min to dislodge the large particles and purified by dialysis (molecular weight cut-off = 1.0 kDa) for 1 day. At last, the product was concentrated by freezing (−50 °C) and drying under vacuum to obtain a pale yellow powder for further characterization and by vacuum drying (50 °C) to gain stock solution for the application.

The pH response experiment was carried out at room temperature using 40 mM Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer (pH 2.0, 2.6, 3.0, 4.0, 4.5, 5.0, 6.1, 7.0, 8.1, 9.0, 10.0, 11.1, 12.0, 12.9, 13.6). Firstly, the stock solution of CDs was diluted 10-fold with ultrapure water and the diluted CDs were applied in the pH sensing assay. Typically, 50 μL of the diluted CDs solution was added to 450 μL of BR buffers. After 15 min, the UV–vis absorption spectra and color evolution were recorded. And the fluorescence spectra were then taken under excitation at 380 nm.

The whole experiment was carried out at room temperature. And the HS-SDME strategy-based assay is displayed in Fig. S1 as an operational flowchart. Before the experiment, the stock solution of CDs was diluted 2-fold with ultrapure water to be prepared for the following fluorescence assay of ammonia. Firstly, 0.9 g (± 0.01 g) of NaOH solid particles was added to a 1.5 mL Eppendorf tube with a cap. And then, 10 μL of 2-fold diluted CDs solution was dropped onto the central position of the internal side of the tube cap. Afterward, 500 μL of NH4Cl standard solution with various concentrations was added to the bottom of the Eppendorf tube to react with NaOH. Subsequently, the cap was closed gently and quickly and the tube was kept standing in the centrifuge tube shelf, which avoided the generated ammonia gas leaking and the CDs drops falling from the tube cap. After 20 min, the ammonia-dissolved CDs drop was pipetted away into a new Eppendorf tube containing 490 μL of ultrapure water to gain a diluted solution. After vigorous stirring, the solution color, UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of the sample (500 μL) were then taken.

The transmission electron microscope (TEM) image reveals the size and morphology of the as-prepared CDs. As can be seen from Fig. 1A, the CDs manifest themselves as spherical nanoparticles with good monodispersity. Moreover, the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image from Fig. 1B reveals high crystallinity of the CDs with a lattice parameter of 0.18 nm, which is similar to the (102) facet of graphitic carbon [18]. The size distribution in Fig. 1C shows that the average size of CDs is 3.43 ± 0.39 nm, obtained by calculating the average size of several hundred nanoparticles.

To obtain insight into the components and structure of the resultant CDs, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum (Fig. 1D) and nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectrum (Fig. 1E) measurements were performed. As depicted in Fig. 1D, the absorption band at 3400 cm−1 corresponds to the O − H stretching vibration. The characteristic absorption band at 2931 cm−1 is due to CH stretching vibration [19]. And the bands in the range of 1704–1632 and 1454–1373 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of CO and C − N, respectively. A peak at 1202 cm−1, which is assigned to C − O stretching vibration, is also observed in the FTIR spectrum. These results indicate that there are plenty of functional groups such as –OH, –COOH, and -NH2 on the surface of CDs. In the 1H NMR spectrum (Fig. 1E), the signals in the range of 1 to 3 ppm are assigned to sp3 C − H protons, and the peaks from 3 to 5 ppm correspond to the protons attached to carbonyl, ether, and hydroxyl groups. The results are in good agreement with those determined by the FTIR spectrum. In addition, the chemical composition of CDs was further confirmed with the X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS). As shown in Fig. 1F, the full-scan spectrum of CDs shows three peaks at 533.1, 400.1, and 285.1 eV, corresponding to O1s, N1s, C1s, respectively. Specifically, the high-resolution C1s spectrum shows the presence of CC bond (C1s at 284.6 eV), sp2 C-N/C-O bonds (285.9 eV), and CO bond (288.6 eV) (Fig. 1G) [20]. In Fig. 1H, the sp3 hybrid nitrogen (N1s, HN bond at 400.5 eV) and pyridinic type nitrogen (C-N-C/C-N bonds at 399.9 eV) can be seen, demonstrating the nitrogen was doped successfully in CDs [21]. Otherwise, the two peaks at 533.2 and 532.2 eV, which are deconvoluted from the O1s spectrum, are corresponding to CO and C − O bonds, respectively (Fig. 1I) [22]. Based on the above analysis results and previous reports [23,24], the fluorescence properties of CDs have a very intimate relationship with the surface groups such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, amine, and amide groups. In addition, the abundant groups not only improve the hydrophilicity and stability of CDs in an aqueous system, but also bring novel properties for the sensing application.

Fig. 2A depicts the absorption and fluorescence spectra of CDs. The peak at 282 nm is attributed to π-π* transition of the conjugated CC bond, and the absorption band near 330–360 nm is due to the n-π* transition of CO bond [25]. The optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of CDs are centered at 380 nm (Ex) and 460 nm (Em), respectively, and CDs show a blue color under a 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 2A, inset). As shown in Fig. 2B, the emission spectra are sensitive to the excitation wavelength. As the excitation wavelength increases from 350 to 440 nm, the emission peak shifts from 445 to 500 nm. This excitation-dependent property is commonly found in fluorescent carbon nanomaterials, which may be affected by the surface state and size effect [26,27]. As providing multicolor fluorescence under different excitation wavelengths, the CDs with the excitation-dependent property will be potentially important for certain practical applications. In addition, the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of the as-prepared CDs was determined to be about 8.3% with quinine sulfate (QY = 0.54) as the standard under an excitation wavelength of 380 nm (Fig. S2) [28].

As displayed in Fig. S3A, the CDs show good stability in different ionic strengths. The fluorescence intensity of CDs is almost unchanged when the concentration of NaCl increases from 0 to 5.00 M. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of CDs shows no obvious decrease by exposing in a 365 nm UV lamp for 90 min, which reveals their good photostability (Fig. S3B). As depicted in Fig. S3C, the CDs also show good stability under various common temperature environments. Moreover, the as-prepared CDs, stored at room temperature for three months, still show strong and steady fluorescence, which demonstrates that the CDs are particularly suitable for long-term use (Fig. S3D). In a word, the excellent salt tolerance, heat resistance, and photostability of CDs guarantee their widespread availability under harsh and sophisticated conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization and process of CDs formation
ce:section.ce:para: In order to optimize the fluorescence performance of CDs, the effects of the synthesis temperature and time have been explored. With increasing temperature (100–200 °C), the absorbance at 283 nm attributed to π-π* transition of CC bond and the absorption band near 320–360 nm owing to n-π* transition of CO bond are heightened [29,30] (Fig. S4A). Correspondingly, the excitation and emission intensities also increase gradually (Fig. S4B). It is inferred that temperature increment promotes CDs growth, which leads to the regular changes of UV–vis and fluorescence spectra. Moreover, the reaction time has also been investigated. As depicted in Fig. S4C and S4D, the longer the reaction time is, the stronger the absorption and fluorescence intensities become. It indicates that the yield of fluorescent CDs is increased by prolonging reaction time. Consequently, considering both the cost/energy conservation and satisfactory fluorescence intensity of CDs, 200 °C and 5 h were selected as the optimal reaction conditions (Fig. S5) [31].

Based on the above results, the possible process of CDs formation was investigated as follows (Fig. 2C): firstly, the polymerization reaction occurred among the various components in onion juice. As the reaction time prolongs, they are then carbonized to form CDs nanocrystalline. And then, the small-sized nanocrystalline gradually increases in size. The surface carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amino groups are formed and endow the CDs with good hydrophilic performance and other features. Although selecting onion as the CDs precursor is not the first method [32,33], we discussed the optimization and the possible formation procedure of onion-derived CDs in detail.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-coalesced colorimetric nanoprobe for wide-range pH sensing
ce:section.ce:para: When we investigated the stability of CDs at different pH values, surprisingly, regular colorimetric responses of CDs to the change in pH values were observed (Fig. 3A, up). Routinely, the color evolution was quantified with UV–vis spectrophotometry first. As depicted in Fig. 3D, the absorbance of CDs solution at 340 nm increases gradually with the increase of pH values from 2.0 to 13.6. The absorption response to pH values is well fitted to a linear model over the broad range of 2.0–12.0 (Fig. 3E). Accordingly, the assay platform is valid for wide-range pH sensing via UV–vis channel.

In addition, the apparent pH-induced color evolution based on CDs can be developed as a naked-eye colorimetric method for pH monitoring. Yet, due to the limited color distinguishing ability of naked eyes, the vast majority of visual chromometry can only realize qualitative or semi-quantitative analysis rather than accurate quantitative detection. To enhance the precision and sensitivity, a smartphone-integrated detector linking biomass CDs was proposed for pH sensing. The bionic electronic eye-driven signal reader of smartphone can accurately capture the color variety, and translate with numerical values. Fig. 3A (down) reveals a more distinct color revolution based on pH variety via smartphone measurement. Thus, it enables accurate and sensitive pH quantitation compared to traditional naked-eye chromometry. Furthermore, the portable analyzer and time-saving procedures make it potential for on-site pH quantitation. As shown in flowsheet (Fig. S6), the color of the sample is record by iPhone 7plus, and then is analyzed with ColorColl app on the smartphone. Fig. 3B shows a captured screen of the running ColorColl app. This app utilizes HSV color model to analysis color. HSV color model, created by A. R. Smith in 1978, translates color information with hue (H), saturation (S), and value (V). The HSV color space simulates the colors organization and conceptualization in human vision, which enables it higher sensitivity and better linear scalability than other spaces like RGB, CMYK, etc. [34]. As depicted in Fig. 3C, V of the HSV model gradually decreases during the pH increasement, with a good linear relationship during the pH range of 6.1–13.6 (inset in Fig. 3C). In order to obtain exact results, every sample was analyzed for five times. Obviously, precise color signals were obtained through the successful utilization of smartphone-integrated biomass CDs sensing platform.

The origin of the colorimetric responses to pH variations was explored by TEM first. As shown in Fig. 4A, the CDs are well separated at pH 2.0. While the pH value is raised, CDs aggregate partially into flower-like nanocluster at pH 6.0, and then gather to big amorphous aggregation at pH 12.0. The pH-driven aggregation of CDs is distinct. Correspondingly, the dynamic light scattering (DLS) was also used to measure the size changes of CDs. As illustrated in Fig. 4B, the hydrodynamic diameters of CDs are ca. 5, 200, and 600 nm at pH 2.0, 6.0, and 12.0, respectively. Because the CDs surface comprises large amounts of –COOH, –OH, and –NH2, these hydrophilic groups can be hydrated considerably, resulting in a large hydrodynamic diameter of CDs. In essence, the variation tendency is consistent with that of TEM tests. In particular, Fig. 4C illustrates that the accumulation of CDs compelled by the increasing pH is a gradual and orderly process. The pH-induced aggregation is also recorded with the UV–vis absorption spectra. As depicted in Fig. 4D, the increasing pH results in a strong enhancement of absorbance at ca. 350–500 nm, indicating the formation of bigger size. Thus, the color of CDs solution evolves from pale yellow to dark yellow with increasing pH (inset of Fig. 4D). Furthermore, we conjecture that the pH-regular aggregation is associated intimately with vivacious groups on CDs surfaces. It is the deprotonated –OH and COOH that strengthen the electron-donating ability of the carbonyl oxygen and carboxyl oxygen, increasing the intermolecular interaction of hydrogen bonding among CDs. Consequently, the CDs gather progressively with increasing pH. The proposed mechanism is illustrated in Fig. 5 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence-channel nanoprobe for wide-range pH sensing
ce:section.ce:para: Next, the pH-regulated fluorescence behavior of the dual-channel sensing system was explored. As shown in Fig. 6A, the fluorescence intensity goes up steadily with increasing pH values from 2.0 to 6.1. Afterwards, the fluorescence intensity exhibits a monotonous decrease from pH 6.1 to 13.6. Therefore, the wide fluorescence detection range is divided into two sections. In the first sensing range of pH 2.0–6.1 (Fig. 6B and C), the fluorescence intensity depends linearly on pH values with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9873, the optimal emission wavelength remains stable at 450 nm (inset of Fig. 6B). During the second pH-response section, while the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreases linearly, the optimal emission wavelength shifts gradually from 450 to 477 nm (Fig. 6D). The calibration plot displays a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.9925) between the fluorescence intensity and pH values in the range of 6.1–13.6 (Fig. 6E). When the solutions at different pH display approximative fluorescence intensities such as pH 2.0 and pH 8.0, it is difficult to distinguish them with fluorescence intensities. However, because higher pH solution shows deeper yellow color, the solutions at pH 2.0 and pH 8.0 can be distinguished by the naked eye. Therefore, it is easy to discriminate them to fulfill accurate pH sensing. In addition, the response stability of CDs nanoprobe is measured over 7 days, and the result reveals that the time has slight influence on the pH-dependent fluorescence intensity (Fig. S7). As shown in Fig. 6F, the reversible process in the pH range 6.0–11.0 can be repeated for six cycles without obvious fatigue. To explore the reversibility of the CDs-based nanoprobe more completely, the pH of the as-prepared CDs was adjusted according to the pH cycle (3.0–6.0-11.0-6.0-3.0) using acid and base as the modulators. Specifically, the fluorescence, absorbance, and morphology of CDs were investigated at the pH values of 3.0, 6.0, and 11.0, respectively. As shown in Fig. S8A and S8B, when the pH is raised from 3.0 to 11.0, the fluorescence intensity increases firstly (pH 3.0–6.0) and then decreases (pH 6.0–11.0). Subsequently, when the pH returns back from 11.0 to 3.0, the fluorescence returns following the original path. Moreover, during the pH cycle, the absorbance of CDs strengthens gradually (pH 3.0–11.0), and then declined (pH 11.0–3.0) (Fig. S8C). The changes in solution color are consistent with that of the absorbance (inset of Fig. S8C). It indicates the good reversibility of both the CDs fluorescence and absorbance. Correspondingly, the TEM images reveal that the increase of pH from 3.0 to 11.0 causes the gradual aggregation of CDs (Fig. S8D). When the pH is lowered to 3.0 from 11.0, the aggregation of CDs disappears and the dispersion recovers. This result further verifies that the pH-induced aggregation and dispersion of CDs create the switch of fluorescence and the change of solution color and absorbance. The good reversibility makes it more applicable and meaningful for pH measurement. Compared with previously reported methods, the biomass CDs-based dual-channel nanoprobe reveals better detection performances including wider linear range, dual-signal response, and more believable results (Table S1). And then, the assay was successfully applied in pH sensing of real water samples (Table S2).

The above analytical applications were completed in optimal conditions. And the optimization of assay conditions was investigated as follows. As shown in Fig. S9A, the fluorescence intensity becomes stable when the reaction time reaches 15 min. Therefore, the incubation time of 15 min was used in the assays. Moreover, the optimization of the concentration of CDs was explored. As shown in Fig. S9B, the optimal concentration of CDs was 10 μL/mL.

According to the colorimetric response mechanism mentioned above, CDs aggregate gradually with the increase of pH values. In the same way, it is a dominant factor to quench the CDs fluorescence under various pH values. Besides the aggregation effect, the fluorescence changes of CDs may have ties with their surface states [35]. To verify the assumption, the average fluorescence lifetime (τ ave) of CDs under different pH conditions were measured (Table S3). As shown in Fig. S10, the average fluorescence lifetime of CDs gradually rises from 4.40 (pH 2.0) to 4.58 ns (pH 6.1), and then declines to 3.78 ns (pH 12.0). The variation tendency is consistent with the change of fluorescence intensity. Thus, the change in the lifetime along with pH variation is associated with the functional groups. With the change of pH values, the protonation/deprotonation of hydroxyl and carbonyl modifies the region of conjugate structure, switching the fluorescence of CDs. In summary, it is the surface states together with the aggregation-induced quenching that governs the increase/decrease of CDs fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual-channel nanoprobe to wide-range ammonia detection
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the pH-dependent colorimetric and fluorescent property of CDs, we further demonstrated the application of CDs to monitor ammonia by combined with HS-SDME technique. As shown in Fig. 7A, when NH4Cl solution is added, NaOH dissolves and reacts with NH4Cl quickly, accompanied by the evolution of heat and alkalization of solution. The as-released heat coupled with strong solution alkalinity prompt almost all the ammonium ions to be transformed into ammonia molecules and then escape from the solution. The escaping ammonia gas can be just captured by the CDs-based nanoprobe. The microextraction process translates NH4 + to ammonia and then extracts it, endowing with a high selectivity for ammonia detection. After absorbing ammonia gas, the CDs solution was alkalized, characterized by the increase of pH values. The pH-induced aggregation leads to color deepening and fluorescence quenching of CDs, which then were recorded with the smartphone and fluorescence spectra. Based on the above process, a novel CDs-based dual-channel assay coupled to HS-SDME strategy for high selective ammonia determination was established.

Relevant sensing conditions were explored in detail. As shown in Fig. S11A, with increasing the amount of NaOH, the CDs fluorescence decreases gradually. When the dosage of NaOH is more than 0.9 g, no obvious decrease of the fluorescence intensity is observed. Moreover, with the NaOH dosage above 1.0 g, the cap will burst open because of the high air pressure, making the solution splashing. Therefore, 0.9 g (± 0.01 g) NaOH was an appropriate dosage of the sensing system. In addition, the optimal interaction time of the CDs solution with ammonia was studied. Fig. S11B shows that a stable fluorescence intensity was obtained after 20 min. Thus, the proposed assay was carried out with a reaction time of 20 min.

As displayed in Fig. 7B, when the concentration of NH4Cl increases, the yellow of CDs solution deepens steadily. The color response of CDs to NH4Cl concentration can be used to quantify ammonia selectively by smartphone-based colorimetry. Fig. 7C illustrates the corresponding smartphone-vivified color graphs. During the NH4Cl concentration is in the range of 0.5–300 mM, V possesses a good linear relationship with the NH4Cl concentration (Fig. 7D). V is the value divided from HSV color model of CDs solution in different pH values. As far as we know, smartphone-integrated colorimetric nanoprobe for selectively quantitative monitoring of ammonia combined with HS-SDME technique has not been reported.

Moreover, the fluorescent performance of CDs was tested under different concentrations of NH4Cl aqueous solution. As depicted in Fig. 7E, the CDs fluorescence gradually decreases with the addition of NH4Cl from 0 to 200 mM. A distinct linear range is shown between the fluorescence intensity difference (F 0 − F) and the concentration of NH4Cl (0.1–50 mM), with a limit of detection (LOD) of 34 μM at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3 (Fig. 7F). The response stability was then investigated. As depicted in Fig. S12, the fluorescence remains largely unchanged after incubating dissolved ammonia for one week, which reveals the satisfactory response stability of CDs-based nanoprobe. Moreover, the selectivity was examined by monitoring fluorescence intensity difference of CDs in the presence of NH4Cl and other common inorganic ions. As presented in Fig. 7G and H, although the other analytes are five times the concentration of the NH4Cl solution, the CDs fluorescence isn't evidently quenched by other species except for NH4Cl, which confirms the outstanding selectivity of this method.

To explore the feasibility and practicality of the developed method, we applied the proposed method to determine ammonia in real water samples. The recovery test was based on the standard addition method. The results listed in Table S4 indicate that the proposed method is practicable and reliable for ammonia determination in real water samples.

In this study, we have successfully built a smartphone-coalesced dual-channel assay for bifunctional pH and ammonia determination based on biomass CDs. The formation process of CDs was monitored. Furthermore, the pH-induced fluorescence, absorption, and color responses of CDs were investigated in detail. The dual-channel transducer demonstrates sensitive pH sensing performances in wide linear ranges of 6.1–13.6, and 2.0–13.6 with colorimetric and fluorescent channels, respectively. The smartphone-coupled colorimetric method demonstrated conspicuously better sensitivity and accuracy than simple naked-eye colorimetric detection. Detailed characterizations proved that the pH-induced aggregation governs the color and fluorescence switch. In addition, combined with the highly selective HS-SDME technique, the CDs were further developed as a nanoprobe for high selective ammonia determination with a wide range. In the view of the diversification of detection channels, precision with the bionic electronic eye, wide response range, low cost, and easy preparation, the CDs-based dual-channel nanoprobe combined with the smartphone has the potential to be used as a portable sensing device for pH and ammonia sensing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21675131), the Municipal Science Foundation of Chongqing City (No. CSTC2015jcyjB50001), and the Innovation Foundation of Chongqing City for Postgraduate (CYB18095).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2019.04.073.
